title: Ensure multi-factor authentication (MFA) is enabled for all IAM users that have a console password

input_row.title: Ensure multi-factor authentication (MFA) is enabled for all IAM users that have a console password
input_row.description: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of authentication assurance beyond traditional credentials. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to the AWS Console, they will be prompted for their user name and password as well as for an authentication code from their physical or virtual MFA token. It is recommended that MFA be enabled for all accounts that have a console password.
input_row.rationale: Enabling MFA provides increased security for console access as it requires the authenticating principal to possess a device that displays a time-sensitive key and have knowledge of a credential.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if a MFA device is enabled for all IAM users having a console password: From Console: 1. Open the IAM console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/. 2. In the left pane, select Users 3. If the MFA or Password age columns are not visible in the table, click the gear icon at the upper right corner of the table and ensure a checkmark is next to both, then click Close. 4. Ensure that for each user where the Password age column shows a password age, the MFA column shows Virtual, U2F Security Key, or Hardware. From Command Line: 1. Run the following command (OSX/Linux/UNIX) to generate a list of all IAM users along with their password and MFA status: Internal Only - General aws iam generate-credential-report aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | cut -d, -f1,4,8 2. The output of this command will produce a table similar to the following: user,password_enabled,mfa_active elise,false,false brandon,true,true rakesh,false,false helene,false,false paras,true,true anitha,false,false 3. For any column having password_enabled set to true , ensure mfa_active is also set to true.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to enable MFA: From Console: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and open the IAM console at 'https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/' 2. In the left pane, select Users. 3. In the User Name list, choose the name of the intended MFA user. 4. Choose the Security Credentials tab, and then choose Manage MFA Device. 5. In the Manage MFA Device wizard, choose Virtual MFA device, and then choose Continue. IAM generates and displays configuration information for the virtual MFA device, including a QR code graphic. The graphic is a representation of the 'secret configuration key' that is available for manual entry on devices that do not support QR codes. 6. Open your virtual MFA application. (For a list of apps that you can use for hosting virtual MFA devices, see Virtual MFA Applications at https://aws.amazon.com/iam/details/mfa/#Virtual_MFA_Applications). If the virtual MFA application supports multiple accounts (multiple virtual MFA devices), choose the option to create a new account (a new virtual MFA device). 7. Determine whether the MFA app supports QR codes, and then do one of the following: Internal Only - General • Use the app to scan the QR code. For example, you might choose the camera icon or choose an option similar to Scan code, and then use the device's camera to scan the code. • In the Manage MFA Device wizard, choose Show secret key for manual configuration, and then type the secret configuration key into your MFA application. When you are finished, the virtual MFA device starts generating one-time passwords. 8. In the Manage MFA Device wizard, in the MFA Code 1 box, type the one-time password that currently appears in the virtual MFA device. Wait up to 30 seconds for the device to generate a new one-time password. Then type the second one-time password into the MFA Code 2 box. 9. Click Assign MFA.
input_row.impact: AWS will soon end support for SMS multi-factor authentication (MFA). New customers are not allowed to use this feature. We recommend that existing customers switch to an alternative method of MFA.
input_row.additional_information: Forced IAM User Self-Service Remediation Amazon has published a pattern that requires users to set up MFA through self-service before they gain access to their complete set of permissions. Until they complete this step, they cannot access their full permissions. This pattern can be used for new AWS accounts. It can also be applied to existing accounts; it is recommended that users receive instructions and a grace period to complete MFA enrollment before active enforcement on existing AWS accounts.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_iam_user_mfa_enabled_for_console_access

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. Use the AWS CLI command `aws iam generate-credential-report` to generate a credential report that includes MFA status for all IAM users.
2. Follow with the command `aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text` to retrieve the report content.
3. Decode the base64 encoded content and parse it to check for users with console password access:
   - The structure contains `user,password_enabled,mfa_active`.
   - Check the `password_enabled` field; for any user with `true`, ensure that the `mfa_active` field is also `true`.
4. Set Pass/Fail criteria:
   - **Pass**: All users with `password_enabled` set to `true` also have `mfa_active` set to `true`.
   - **Fail**: Any user with `password_enabled` set to `true` but `mfa_active` set to `false`. 
5. Example validation logic:
   - After decoding, you can insert logic to check each row in the parsed data. Each user entry that meets the criteria for `password_enabled` being true and `mfa_active` being false indicates a failure of the control.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The use of AWS CLI commands to generate and retrieve a credential report is a valid automated method to assess the MFA status of IAM users.
- This check can indeed be performed using AWS APIs, specifically through the AWS CLI as described, and can also be implemented using the boto3 library in Python to automate the process further.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9
- The automation details are technically accurate. The use of `aws iam generate-credential-report` and `aws iam get-credential-report` commands is correct for generating and retrieving the credential report.
- The decoding and parsing logic is correctly described to check the `password_enabled` and `mfa_active` fields.
- The program name follows a reasonable convention but could be more descriptive. It is clear but could be improved to reflect the specific check being performed.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- **Program Name:** Consider renaming the program to `aws_iam_user_mfa_status_check_for_console_access` for better clarity and descriptiveness.
- **Missing Considerations:** It would be beneficial to include error handling for scenarios where the credential report generation might fail or when the report is not immediately available (as it can take some time to generate).
- **Alternative Methods:** While the CLI method is effective, using boto3 with `generate_credential_report()` and `get_credential_report()` methods could provide more flexibility, especially in larger automation scripts or integrations with other systems.
- **Additional Checks:** Consider including a check for IAM roles or service accounts that might also have console access, though this is less common.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-founded, and the technical approach is sound. The use of AWS CLI commands is appropriate for this type of compliance check.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- **Modify:** Update the program name to `aws_iam_user_mfa_status_check_for_console_access` for improved clarity.
- Ensure error handling is in place for the credential report generation and retrieval process.
- Consider providing an alternative implementation using boto3 for environments where Python automation is preferred.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using AWS CLI commands to generate and retrieve a credential report. This report includes the necessary fields to determine if MFA is enabled for IAM users with console passwords. The AWS CLI and boto3 provide the necessary capabilities to automate this check effectively, making manual inspection unnecessary.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_iam_user_mfa_status_check_for_console_access

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS Resource/Service:** IAM Users
- **API/CLI Method:** 
  - `aws iam generate-credential-report`
  - `aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text`
- **Properties to Check:** `user`, `password_enabled`, `mfa_active`
- **Pass Criteria:** All users with `password_enabled` set to `true` also have `mfa_active` set to `true`.
- **Fail Criteria:** Any user with `password_enabled` set to `true` but `mfa_active` set to `false`.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There were no disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported an automated approach using AWS CLI commands. The second opinion suggested a more descriptive program name, which has been adopted.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Amazon_Web_Services_Foundations_Benchmark_v4.0.1
